schools.summary: Departure from Judea was prudent humility — pacifying envy, not avoiding destiny; Christ's omniscience is stressed.
Jesus knew all things as God; strategic retreat before the appointed time.
Text-critical note: Lord vs. Jesus; departure tied to Pharisee jealousy of growing popularity.

debates.question: What prompts the withdrawal — fear, humility, or providence?

sides.position: Prudential avoidance: time to die had not come
Pacifying envy and preventing false inference about his body

schools.summary: Baptism's validity rests not on the minister but on Christ the Author — ministers are subordinate agents.
Clarifies that Jesus himself did not baptize, correcting the report reaching the Pharisees; disciples assisted.

outliers.note: Notes this is the only NT instance of kaítoige (and yet indeed), marking the parenthetic correction as forceful.

schools.summary: Departure timed to avoid Pharisee confrontation; route toward Galilee confirmed; text-critical note on 'again'.
Samaria lay directly between Judea and Galilee; the usual route for Galileans went through it; Josephus confirms this.
Historical background of Samaritan mixed origins; theological purpose in going through Samaria.

debates.question: Does 'he must needs' imply geographical necessity or divine appointment?

sides.position: Simply geographical — no other road was practicable
Also providential — the encounter was purposed

schools.summary: Sychar = Shechem (ancient Sichem); Jacob's gift to Joseph (Gen 48:22); detailed notes on location near Ebal/Gerizim.

debates.question: Is Sychar identical with Shechem or a variant form?

sides.position: Sychar is a variant form of Shechem
The form was already in popular use by John's time

schools.summary: Jacob's well identified archaeologically; Jesus's weariness is real human exhaustion from noon journey; distinction between pege (spring) and phrear (cistern) noted.
The conversation arose naturally from circumstance; Jesus thirsts for her faith as well as water.
Woman is from the country of Samaria, not the city; the request opens the dialogue naturally.
Jesus availed himself of the opportunity for instruction — circumstance becomes occasion for grace.
Disciples absent to buy food; Jesus alone created the opportunity; tension with Jewish prohibition on Samaritan food noted.

outliers.note: Raises the Jewish prohibition on eating Samaritan food and flags tension with disciples going to buy from them.

schools.summary: Jewish-Samaritan enmity grounded in history; textual note on syngchraomai; woman's response is half-scoffing.
The woman disdains Christ initially; her remark is a retort to perceived Jewish contempt.

outliers.note: Argues the Greek overstates; Jews had commercial dealings with Samaritans but avoided social friendship.

schools.summary: Gift of God = either Christ himself (John 3:16) or the gracious opportunity; living water = flowing water, but Jesus means the Spirit and grace.
Astonishing condescension to offer grace to one so undeserving.
The stress lies on 'thou wouldst have asked of him' — faith and asking are the required response.

debates.question: What is 'the gift of God' here?

sides.position: Christ himself, the Father's gift to the world
The gracious opportunity now being offered to her

schools.summary: Woman's misunderstanding is literal: he has no bucket, the well is deep; living water taken as spring water — natural misreading.
Woman's tone has shifted to respect; her misunderstanding is common human limitation.
'Art thou greater than our father Jacob?' — Greek expects negative answer; Samaritan claim to descent from Jacob via Joseph.
The comparison reveals the smallness of her conception; Jesus does not deny being greater — he implies it.
Jesus leads the woman by degrees from natural to spiritual; the contrast is between material and spiritual sustenance.
Greek tense distinction: present participle vs. aorist subjunctive — physical water requires repeated drinking; spiritual water quenches permanently.
'Shall never thirst' = strongest double negative; water is the Spirit and grace; inner spring vs. external supply.
If thirst returns it is the person's fault, not the water's; the spring of grace continually supplies.
Cyril of Jerusalem reads the living water as the Spirit given in baptism, drawing on John 7:37-39.

outliers.note: Applies the living water explicitly to the Spirit of baptism, reading through John 7:37–39.

schools.summary: Woman still misunderstands — takes living water literally; she desires relief from daily water-carrying rather than spiritual renewal.
Her request shows she has not yet allowed Christ's authority to penetrate; only when sin is exposed will genuine desire awaken.
Go call thy husband — Jesus shifts method to convict conscience; a deliberate change of approach.
Christ does not abandon her but takes another method; he strikes directly at her bosom sin.
'I have no husband' — true but evasive; aner means both man and husband, so she veils her situation with technically true language.
The stroke has gone deeper — woman does not leave in anger; her answer shows a conscience newly touched.
Five husbands: either widowed successively, or divorced; current man is not legally her husband; Clarke allows she may have been legally married to all five.
Lightfoot reads serial adultery; Christ stops her mouth with the evidence of her own life.

sides.position: She had been legitimately married five times, divorced through misbehavior
Adultery and serial divorce are implied

schools.summary: Woman's admiration is raised; she receives the rebuke and does not depart; recognition of prophet is fruit of reproof.
Conviction opens the heart to instruction; she is now ready and requests it.
Woman gestures to Mount Gerizim; historical background of the Samaritan schism; patriarchal worship at Shechem.
She passes from the particular to the general — from her sin to the great question of true worship.

debates.question: Is the question about worship an evasion of the moral challenge?

sides.position: It is an evasion, a change of subject to avoid personal discomfort
She genuinely moves to the deepest religious question she knows

schools.summary: 'Believe me' — unique address; announces the abolition of local, ceremonial worship; the hour is coming.
The ceremonial law is set aside; the particular, fixed place of worship prescribed by Moses was temporary.
Chrysostom: this teaching requires faith before everything — without it, the great doctrines cannot be received.

outliers.note: Opens his homily with an extended meditation on faith as the necessary precondition for receiving this teaching.

schools.summary: Samaritan worship defective because they rejected the Prophets; Jewish worship superior; salvation is of the Jews = Messiah comes through Israel.
Samaritan religion is superstitious for rejecting the prophets; but the ceremonial distinctions are shortly to be abolished.
'And now is' added — present spiritual independence begins immediately; true worshippers worship in reality, not just ceremonially.
Christ abolishes external ceremonies; the epoch of spiritual worship has begun.
True worshippers worship at all times and in all places — no longer geographically restricted.

outliers.note: Reads spirit and truth pneumatologically — the Holy Spirit given in baptism is the agent of true worship; cites John 3:5.

schools.summary: 'God is spirit' — anarthrous predicate; parallel to God is Light (1 John 1:5) and God is Love (1 John 4:8); non-corporeality of God.
Human flesh delights in external forms; God's spiritual nature requires spiritual worship — the argument from divine nature.
God is Spirit = full of all spiritual perfections; worship must be by faith and love, not bodies and places.
Samaritans expected a Messiah based on Deut 18:18 (prophet like Moses); Lightfoot examines how they knew the term without the Prophets.
Her expectation is the fruit of Mosaic deposit; now she is prepared to refer all to the Messiah.
Chrysostom: the woman is struck with astonishment at the loftiness of his teaching and recognizes the one who will tell us all things.

outliers.note: Examines how Samaritans knew the term Messiah if they rejected the Prophets — argues the Pentateuch alone (esp. Deut 18:18) was sufficient.

schools.summary: First explicit, open Messianic self-declaration; Jesus spoke most plainly here, not to Jews; safer to do so in Samaria.
Jesus presents himself as her Teacher in compliance with her expectation; proceeds to fuller instruction.
Disciples marvelled at Jesus speaking with a woman in public — contrary to rabbinic custom; their restraint shows respect.
Imperfect tense: disciples kept marvelling; the woman departed as soon as they came.
Abandoned waterpot = haste and excitement; first aorist indicates she left it immediately; the waterpot as pledge of return and mark of firsthand witness.
The nature of faith is that when we become partakers of eternal life, we wish to bring others to share.
'Come, see a man who told me all things I did' — slight exaggeration; Greek meti question invites inquiry rather than asserting.
She speaks modestly to leave them to their own inquiry — she invites, not dictates.

debates.question: Is her testimony hesitant or confident?

sides.position: Already convinced but speaks modestly to invite others to judge for themselves
Still uncertain and includes herself in the inquiry

schools.summary: 'Went out' (aorist, effective) vs. 'were coming' (imperfect, continuous): the Samaritans left at once and were still on the way during the conversation of vv.31-38.
Disciples offer food out of care; Christ was fatigued; their request is simple and affectionate.
Imperfect tense: disciples kept beseeching him; Rabbi is the correct translation of the address.
'Meat/food ye know not of' — emphatic pronoun contrast: I vs. ye; the food is doing the Father's will.
Not a permanent principle of asceticism but a specific instance of zeal that overrides hunger when a soul is being won.
Disciples wonder if someone has brought food supernaturally; the negative particle expects no but they cannot be sure.
'My meat is to do the will' — two purpose clauses: that I may do the will and finish the work; Christ's Messianic consciousness is clear and steady.
Calvin: doing God's will is not merely highly valued but the supreme delight — as David valued the Law.
Wesley: 'My meat — that which satisfies the strongest appetite of my soul.'
Material harvest is four months away; spiritual harvest is now — the Samaritans approaching are the whitening fields.
'Four months' may be a common proverb or a specific calendar note; either way, the spiritual harvest does not wait.

debates.question: Is 'four months to harvest' a proverb or a calendar note?

sides.position: A rustic proverb about the interval from sowing to harvest
A specific chronological note placing the scene in December

schools.summary: Reaper receives wages and gathers fruit for eternal life; sower and reaper rejoice together — present harvest removes the usual separation.
Calvin: a large and most excellent reward is reserved for our labor — the motive for missionary diligence.
The proverb 'one soweth, another reapeth' is verified in the spiritual realm; known in Greek and Hebrew literature.
Calvin: the disciples reap what the prophets sowed; this was not resented because the laborers shared the same cause.
'I sent you to reap' — emphatic I; the disciples enter into others' labors: the prophets, John the Baptist, Christ himself have been the sowers.
Origen: many believing on one woman's word shows the Spirit's harvest work.
Many Samaritans believed because of the saying of the woman who testified; her faithful witness was effective.
Calvin: the word is used inaccurately — initial faith was faith in the woman's word, soon deepened by hearing Jesus himself.
Chrysostom: the Samaritans' superiority to the Jews is shown by their reception of Christ; envy and malice destroyed Jewish advantage.
Jesus abode two days with the Samaritans; no recorded miracles — they believed his word alone.
'Many more believed' — pollō pleiones (more by much); Jesus's direct ministry exceeded the woman's witness numerically.
Calvin: example teaches that we ought never to refrain from working when opportunity is given.
'Not because of thy talk but we have ourselves heard' — transition from testimony-based to direct-encounter faith; lalia (talk) vs. direct word.
Calvin: they acknowledge greater authority to Christ's own word; her witness had led them to the source.
Augustine addresses the seeming contradiction: Jesus leaves Samaria for Galilee where a prophet has no honor — but proceeds to receive honor there.
The proverb applies to Nazareth specifically, not all Galilee; Jesus avoids Nazareth and goes to Cana where he is received.

debates.question: Why does John say Jesus went to Galilee where a prophet has no honor, if the Galileans received him?

sides.position: The proverb applies specifically to Nazareth, not all Galilee
The honor they gave was shallow and based on miracles, not true faith

schools.summary: The Evangelist quotes the proverb as explanation of why Jesus avoided Nazareth and went to Cana; John's editorial note.
Galileans received him because they had seen his Jerusalem miracles at the Passover (John 2:23); Calvin: uncertain how long this honor lasted.
Jesus returns to Cana to confirm and deepen the faith his first miracle produced.
Nobleman (basilikos) = officer of Herod's court; Capernaum a day's journey away; the scene is set for a distance miracle.
Gregory: the nobleman's faith was deficient — he thought Jesus must come down to heal; the miracle will correct this.

sides.position: An officer of Herod Antipas's court
A member of the Herodian family or royal descent

schools.summary: Nobleman hears of Jesus's arrival and travels twenty miles to ask for healing; faith is present but limited by belief in bodily necessity.
Calvin: he limits Christ's manner of granting assistance by connecting power to physical presence — a common form of weak faith.
The rebuke is addressed to the plural ye — the Galileans collectively; the contrast with sign-free Samaritan faith is made explicit.
Adam Clarke: the nobleman had faith but not enough — the rebuke corrects its insufficiency.
Nobleman does not argue but renews his plea; the urgency of 'ere my child die' shows love and desperation.
Calvin: Christ does not altogether reject him — the rebuke was corrective; perseverance in prayer is evidence of faith.
'Go thy way; thy son liveth' — a kind and tender address; the man believes on the word before any sign is seen.
Calvin: the astonishing kindness and condescension of Christ — he stretches his power beyond what was expected.
Genitive absolute construction; servants confirmed 'your boy lives' using present tense; the faith of John 4:50 is confirmed before arrival.
Calvin: the effect of faith together with the efficacy of the word — as Christ by a word restores, so in one moment the father regains his son.
The inquiry about the hour confirms the miracle; 'seventh hour' = 1pm; kompsoteron eschen is a medical Greek term for taking a turn for the better.
Calvin: God provides the inquiry by divine impulse, so that the truth of the miracle might be rendered conspicuous.
The father knew (came to know) that it was the same hour; 'himself believed' — aorist, complete faith in Jesus himself; whole household converts.
Calvin: the earlier belief (v.50) was trust in the word; this is full personal faith in Jesus himself as Messiah.

debates.question: How does v.53 'he believed' relate to v.50 'the man believed'?

sides.position: V.50 was trust in the word; v.53 is complete personal faith in Jesus himself
V.53 refers to fuller assurance and household conversion

schools.summary: 'This is again the second miracle' — the qualifier 'coming out of Judea into Galilee' restricts the count to the Galilean series; not the second miracle overall.
Chrysostom: every word of Scripture is precious and should not be passed over without attention.
